content.text: What are Cross-Tenant Access Settings?

Cross-Tenant Access Settings (CTAS) give you precise control over how external identities from other Microsoft Entra tenants can access your resources, and how your users can access resources in other tenants. Settings operate at two levels: a default policy that covers all external tenants, and per-partner overrides for specific tenants.

CTAS applies to all Microsoft Entra-backed sign-ins across tenant boundaries — not just guest invitations. It covers B2B Collaboration, B2B Direct Connect, Microsoft Cloud settings, and inbound trust controls.
CTAS vs External Collaboration Settings
Feature
External Collaboration Settings
Cross-Tenant Access Settings
Controls
Who in your org can send invitations
Which external tenants can access your resources
Granularity
Org-wide only
Default + per-partner overrides
Trust claims
Not applicable
MFA, compliant device, hybrid AAD join
Scope
Outbound invitations
Inbound and outbound access, direct connect
Admin path
External Identities → External collaboration settings
External Identities → Cross-tenant access settings
Reading the Current CTAS Policy
Use the Graph API to read both the default policy and all per-partner configurations. The default policy applies to any tenant not explicitly listed in the partners collection.

# Read current default CTAS policy
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.Read.All','Policy.ReadWrite.CrossTenantAccess'

# Get default policy
$default = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/crossTenantAccessPolicy/default'
$default | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 6

# List all per-partner configurations
$partners = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/crossTenantAccessPolicy/partners'
$partners.value | ForEach-Object {
    Write-Host "Partner tenant: $($_.tenantId) | B2B collab inbound: $($_.b2bCollaborationInbound.usersAndGroups.accessType)"
}
Configuring the Default Policy

The default policy acts as a catchall for all tenants not explicitly configured. The most common secure baseline is to block inbound B2B collaboration by default and only open specific partner tenants as needed.
Adding a Per-Partner Configuration

Per-partner entries override the default for a specific tenant. Add the partner's tenant ID, then configure inbound access and trust settings. The partner tenant ID can be found via their Entra ID overview or by asking them to share it.
Restricting Outbound Access

Outbound settings control which of YOUR users can access resources in other tenants. Use group-scoped outbound rules to prevent data exfiltration — only members of an approved group can access the external tenant.
B2B Direct Connect for Teams Shared Channels

B2B Direct Connect enables Teams Shared Channels between tenants. Unlike B2B Collaboration, no guest object is created — the external user accesses the channel directly using their home tenant credentials. Both tenants must configure inbound and outbound direct connect.
Inbound Trust Settings

Trust settings tell Entra ID whether to accept claims (MFA completion, device compliance) that were evaluated at the partner tenant. Without trust, Conditional Access re-evaluates those claims at your tenant, which can force re-authentication.
Trust Setting
Effect When Enabled
When to Enable
isMfaAccepted
Skip MFA re-prompt if partner verified MFA
Partner has equivalent MFA policy
isCompliantDeviceAccepted
Trust partner's Intune compliance state
Partner is managed by Intune with equivalent policies
isHybridAzureADJoinedDeviceAccepted
Trust partner's hybrid AAD join state
Partner has on-premises AD with AAD Connect
Trusting partner MFA does not bypass Conditional Access — it satisfies the MFA grant control using the partner's evaluation. The CA policy still evaluates all other conditions (location, app, risk).
Auditing CTAS Changes and Blocked Sign-ins
Track policy changes via Audit Logs and identify sign-ins blocked by CTAS through sign-in error codes 53003, 53011, and 530021. These indicate the cross-tenant access policy denied the request.

// Audit CTAS policy changes
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where Category == 'Policy'
| where OperationName has 'crossTenantAccessPolicy'
| extend
    ChangedBy = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName),
    PolicyTarget = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName),
    ResultStatus = tostring(Result)
| project TimeGenerated, OperationName, ChangedBy, PolicyTarget, ResultStatus
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Find sign-ins from external tenants that were blocked by CTAS
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where ResultType in ('53003','53011','530021')  // CTAS-related error codes
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, HomeTenantId = HomeTenantId,
          AppDisplayName, ResultType, ResultDescription
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Infrastructure as Code with Terraform
The azuread Terraform provider includes dedicated resources for CTAS. Manage the default policy and per-partner overrides in version-controlled configuration alongside other identity infrastructure.
CTAS Configuration Checklist
Step
Action
Priority
Set default inbound to 'blocked' to require explicit partner allowlisting
High
Add per-partner entries for all active B2B collaboration relationships
High
Configure inbound trust only for partners with equivalent security policies
Medium
Restrict outbound to approved groups to prevent unsanctioned data sharing
Medium
Enable B2B Direct Connect only for Teams shared channel partners
Low
Set up Audit Log alerts for CTAS policy changes
High
Review per-partner entries quarterly to remove inactive relationships
Medium
Bulk Configuring Multiple Partner Tenants

When onboarding many partners at once — such as after a merger or a new supply chain relationship — bulk-configure CTAS from a CSV file. Each row specifies a partner tenant ID, whether inbound access is allowed, and whether to trust their MFA claims.
Tenant Restrictions v2 (TRv2)

Tenant Restrictions v2 extends CTAS enforcement to managed devices and proxies. Even if a user navigates directly to login.microsoftonline.com, TRv2 prevents them from signing in to any external tenant not in the allowlist. It requires a proxy that injects specific HTTP headers on AAD authentication endpoints.
TRv2 differs from CTAS inbound controls: CTAS controls who can enter your tenant; TRv2 controls which tenants YOUR users can leave to. Together they provide bidirectional boundary enforcement.
Detecting CA Policy Gaps for External Sign-ins
Use KQL to find sign-ins from external tenants where no Conditional Access policy was applied, or where MFA was not satisfied. These represent gaps in your CTAS and CA coverage.

// Identify Conditional Access gaps for external tenant sign-ins
// Find sign-ins from external tenants that had no CA policy applied
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where UserType == 'Guest'
| where isempty(ConditionalAccessPolicies) or array_length(ConditionalAccessPolicies) == 0
| summarize
    SignInCount = count(),
    Users       = dcount(UserPrincipalName),
    Apps        = dcount(AppDisplayName)
    by HomeTenantId
| order by SignInCount desc

// Find external tenant sign-ins where MFA was NOT satisfied
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where UserType == 'Guest'
| where AuthenticationRequirement == 'singleFactorAuthentication'
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, HomeTenantId, AppDisplayName, AuthenticationRequirement
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Removing a Partner Configuration

When a business relationship ends, remove the per-partner CTAS entry so the tenant falls back to the (typically restrictive) default policy. Also clean up any guest user objects from that tenant to close residual access paths.

# Remove a per-partner CTAS entry (partner relationship ended)
$partnerTenantId = "aaaabbbb-0000-1111-2222-333344445555"

# Verify the partner entry exists
try {
    $entry = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
        -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/crossTenantAccessPolicy/partners/$partnerTenantId"
    Write-Host "Entry found: $($entry.tenantId)"
} catch {
    Write-Host 'No entry found for this partner tenant'
    return
}

# Delete the entry — tenant falls back to the default policy
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method DELETE \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/crossTenantAccessPolicy/partners/$partnerTenantId"
Write-Host 'Partner CTAS entry removed. Default policy now applies.'

# Also revoke any active guest user objects from this tenant
Get-MgUser -Filter "userType eq 'Guest'" -All |
    Where-Object { $_.Mail -like '*@fabrikam.com' } |
    ForEach-Object { Remove-MgUser -UserId $_.Id; Write-Host "Removed: $($_.UserPrincipalName)" }
Common CTAS Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Default policy left at 'allow all'
Any Entra-backed identity can access your resources
Set default inbound to 'blocked'
Trusting MFA without verifying partner policy
Weak partner MFA (SMS) satisfies your CA MFA requirement
Trust only partners with equivalent MFA strength
Not configuring outbound restrictions
Your users can exfiltrate data to personal tenants
Scope outbound to approved groups or block by default
Using B2B Direct Connect without CA
External Teams members have no CA enforcement
Apply CA policy to direct connect sign-ins
Forgetting to remove partner entries
Inactive tenants retain access after relationship ends
Quarterly review of partner entries with expiry audit
Summary
CTAS provides two-dimensional control: inbound (who can access your tenant) and outbound (where your users can go).
A default policy covers all tenants; per-partner overrides let you selectively open or further restrict specific relationships.
Inbound trust settings (isMfaAccepted, isCompliantDeviceAccepted) reduce re-authentication friction for well-governed partners.
B2B Direct Connect is distinct from B2B Collaboration — it requires both tenant operators to configure it and creates no guest object.
The secure default is to block inbound B2B at the default level and add explicit partner allowlist entries as relationships are established.
Audit CTAS changes via the Policy category in Audit Logs, and use error codes 53003/53011/530021 to detect CTAS-blocked sign-in attempts.
